meta.title: Drift — Nomad Super App
meta.subtitle: A design concept exploring what happens when city discovery, remote jobs, and IRL community are structured around one place — not three separate platforms.

data.headline: A city isn't a location. It's a decision.
data.body: When a digital nomad considers relocating, they're not asking 'where is Prague?' They're asking: Can I afford it? Is there work? Will there be people like me? These three questions currently live on three different platforms. This concept explores what happens when you structure them around the city — not the task — and what design decisions that forces you to make.

data.title: Each screen answers one thing a nomad needs before booking.

items.title: Score first, description later.
items.body: The city view surfaces Score (9.2) and Cost ($3,200/mo) before any descriptive text. The hypothesis: nomads decide on cities quantitatively first, qualitatively second. The description exists for confirmation, not discovery.

items.title: Four currencies. One surface.
items.body: Payment options (USD · Euro · BTC · ETH) appear as equal pill badges on the job card — not inside the detail view. For a freelancer whose income is already in crypto, this is a qualifying signal that needs to be visible before the click.

items.title: Who's going before what it is.
items.body: 'Nomad joined' appears before 'About the event'. The hypothesis: the decision to attend an IRL event is social before it's informational. 99 nomads already there answers 'is this my crowd?' faster than any description.

items.title: Payment options on the card, not the detail
items.description: For a crypto-earning freelancer, payment type is a qualifying criterion — not a detail. Burying BTC/ETH inside the job detail means opening 20 listings to find one that pays the right way. Moving payment pills to the card surface lets users filter by scanning. The tradeoff: four pills consume card real estate that could show more of the role description. The bet is that payment type matters more to this user than two extra lines of requirements.

items.title: Social proof above event description
items.description: Nomads don't attend events for the content — they attend to meet people. '99+ nomads joined' appears before 'About the event' because community fit is the first question, event format is the second. The tradeoff is real: someone unfamiliar with the event has to scroll past the social proof to understand what they're showing up to. The count badge (12 Cad, June 12) does some of that work, but description-first would serve newcomers better.

items.title: City as a content hub, not a static profile
items.description: Events and jobs aren't useful in the abstract — they're useful because they're in Prague. Most platforms strip that location context away. Keeping events and jobs under the city tab (Event 3 · Job 20) makes the relationship explicit: you're not browsing the job board, you're seeing what work looks like in this specific city. The risk: tabs require users to discover they exist. The count badges are doing critical signalling work. Without them, the tabs look empty.

items.name: The crypto-native nomad
items.type: Composite · based on app's evident target user
items.biography: Earns in USDC or ETH. Moves cities every two to three months. Evaluates relocations the way some people evaluate apartments — score, cost, who's already there. Finds community through Bitcoin meetups and Telegram groups, not LinkedIn events.
items.goal: Find a city with a livable cost, an active crypto freelance market, and enough nomads around that the first two weeks don't feel isolating.
items.needs: Quantitative city signals upfront. Crypto payment visible without digging through job descriptions. Events with social proof of who's attending — not just what they are.
items.frustrations: Opening 15 job listings to find the one that pays in crypto. City research scattered across four tabs that don't talk to each other. Arriving in a city and discovering the nomad community a week after the best meetup.
items.why_use: Opens City Profile, checks Score and Cost before reading a word of the description, scans Job 20 to gauge market depth, looks at Event 3 to see what's coming up. The numbers on the tabs are the entire first pass.

data.text: The best city for a nomad isn't the cheapest or the most beautiful. It's the one where you already know someone before you land.
